notes: Repository signals support both `react-router` and `remix`. The primary candidate was selected conservatively from stronger structural evidence.

The repository directly uses React Router 7: package scripts invoke `react-router build` and `react-router typegen`, Vite loads `@react-router/dev/vite`, and application entry points import from `react-router` and `react-router/dom`.

The `app/root.tsx`, `app/entry.client.tsx`, `app/entry.server.tsx`, and `app/routes` layout resembles Remix conventions, but no Remix package, config, or Vite plugin is evidenced; React Router is the conservative canonical identity.
The repository is a single TypeScript application with Vite, Bun (`bun.lock` and `packageManager`), React 19 rendering, and routes rooted at `app/routes`.
Article source and support code are rooted under `app/articles`, including `content`, `registry.ts`, authors, resources, and SEO configuration.
Keep the candidate understanding. It matches the deterministic base at the same repository SHA.
React Router 7 is directly evidenced by the react-router package and CLI scripts, @react-router/dev Vite plugin, react-router.config.ts, and application imports from react-router/react-router-dom.
Remix-like app structure is a legacy-compatible React Router 7 convention, while no Remix package or Remix-specific build configuration is present.
Bun, Vite, TypeScript, React 19, app/routes, and app/articles are all directly supported by manifest, lockfile, configuration, and repository layout evidence.

article_surface_resolution.system_type: React Router registry-driven TSX article system
evidence_summary: app/routes/articles.index.tsx imports getArticlesSortedNewestFirst and resolveArticleRouteSlug from ~/articles/registry.
app/routes/articles.slug.tsx imports getArticleBySlug from ~/articles/registry and uses ArticleLayout.
Route evidence identifies app/routes/articles.index.tsx as /articles and app/routes/articles.slug.tsx as the /articles/* detail surface.
The documented content pattern is app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx, with numerous TSX article modules present in category directories.

No supplied publish-mutation candidate is proven to be the universal safe target for new entries: the system spans category directories, while registry mapping and safety readiness are reported false.
article_surface_resolution.why_this_is_article_system: The /articles listing route imports article registry functions from ~/articles/registry, while the /articles detail route resolves articles through the same registry and renders ArticleLayout. Route evidence explicitly connects these surfaces to article modules under app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx. The app/articles candidate is the supplied system-level locator containing the registry and article support files.
why_other_candidates_lost: app/articles/content/featured contains many article-like TSX files, but it is only one category subtree and cannot safely represent the full multi-category article system.
app/articles/content/careers is likewise a category-specific article subtree rather than the canonical system root.
app/articles/content/featured/page.tsx is a listing-like page, not the primary /articles listing surface linked to the registry-driven detail route.
app/routes/resources.tsx redirects to /articles and is therefore not an independent canonical listing surface.
app/components/articles contains rendering components, not stored article entries.

The /articles listing and /articles/* detail routes are well supported by explicit React Router and registry evidence, but the retained primary content_source is app/articles while the route evidence identifies the actual article-module pattern as app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx. The supplied app/articles candidate contains registry/support files rather than the article entry modules, and no supplied universal publish target is proven safe. Therefore the overall article system exists, but the primary resolution is not sufficiently internally consistent to keep conservatively.

runContext.design_guide:
# MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 — Scope: branding, Tailwind tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot article generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, standalone page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** application. Observed versions: ```txt react: 19.2.1 react-router: 7.10.0 vite: 7.3.1 typescript: 5.9.3 tailwindcss: 4.1.17 ``` Use React Router links: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles ``` Do not use Next.js APIs or imports: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do not use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue or Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content modules export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* Article body only. */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`.** The route handler/layout already renders article framing. - Do not add duplicate article titles, hero sections, breadcrumbs, dates, or author sections when the layout renders them. - Content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If a wrapper is necessary, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article components rather than ad-hoc card, callout, or image markup. - Do not use placeholder services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, supported component fallback behaviour, or `` only in templates. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` unless explicitly requested. - Registry route slugs and SEO-config paths must match exactly. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The article system combines MLAI CSS variables with bold article surfaces. | Token / colour | Usage | Role | |---|---|---| | `--brand` | `ArticleStepList` bullets and borders | MLAI teal accent | | `--brand-ink` | Link hover states | Teal link colour | | `--accent` | Step-list bullet text | Dark text on brand surfaces | | `--soft` | Existing legacy `ArticleCallout` styling | Soft teal panel | | `--brutalist-orange` | Article search filters | Orange token | | `--brutalist-purple` | Article search filters | Purple token | | `--brutalist-black` | Article search filters | Black token | | `--brutalist-yellow` | Article search filters | Yellow token | | `--brutalist-beige` | `HeroSection` beige variant | Beige page surface | | `#ff3d00` | Audience cards, quotes, CTAs | Orange | | `#4b1bd1` | Audience cards and quotes | Purple | | `#ffe900` | Audience cards | Yellow | | `#2b0f70` | Resource CTA | Deep purple | | `#0d9488` | Resource CTA | Teal/cyan | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline underline-offset-4` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article content wrappers. - Invent new colours or variants for article design-system components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` in generated article content. - Overuse bright surfaces in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utilities and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup Tailwind scans application code under `app/` and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography' import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss' export default { content: ['./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [typography], } satisfies Config ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components for generated article work. ### 4.2 Import convention Use the configured `~/` alias for application imports: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when modifying an existing nearby module that already uses them. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`; use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use existing article design-system components. Do not recreate their functionality with raw bordered cards, quote boxes, or image blocks. ### 6.1 Persona and audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed card variants: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code audience cards with raw `
` border or shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotations. ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use unsupported generated-content variants: ```tsx ``` Do not use `ArticleCallout` for newly generated article callouts. Use `QuoteBlock`. --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw `` markup. ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` `ArticleImageBlock` uses `ImageWithFallback` and `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` internally. Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: 'Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should retain human review, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters.', }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Do not add an identical heading directly before it. Supported item keys: ```ts export type ArticleFAQItem = { q?: string question?: string a?: ReactNode answer?: ReactNode } ``` Prefer `question` and `answer`. Keep answers as simple inline content because the component renders answers inside a paragraph. --- ### 6.5 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList`. ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` For generated content, use `accent="teal"`: ```tsx ``` Steps may be strings or `{ label: string }` objects. Prefer strings for standard generated articles. --- ### 6.6 Table-of-contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only where the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. ```tsx import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` ```tsx ``` For no external margin: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.7 References Use `ArticleReferences`. ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Supported categories: ```tsx 'government' | 'guide' | 'analysis' | 'industry' | 'watchlist' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleReferencesProps = { references: ReferenceItem[] heading?: string description?: string headingId?: string previewCount?: number } ``` --- ### 6.8 Resource lists Use `ArticleResourceList`. ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference unavailable components: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` Keep CTA copy generic. Do not hard-code product names unless explicitly requested. #### Resource CTA ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to a registered article. ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` Supported props: ```ts type Props = { slug: string ctaText?: string className?: string descriptionOverride?: string variant?: 'default' | 'compact' authorOverride?: ArticleAuthor } ``` The component resolves article metadata and route slugs internally. --- ### 6.11 Footer article navigation Use `ArticleFooterNav` only where the article pattern needs in-body footer navigation and the layout does not already provide it. ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleFooterNavProps = { backHref?: string backLabel?: string topHref?: string className?: string } ``` --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only when an in-body author section is required and the layout does not already render author information. ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Article content files should not manually render an article hero/header because `ArticleLayout` handles article framing. Do not import: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap content in: ```tsx ... ``` Do not render `ArticleHeroHeader` in generated article body files unless explicitly requested for a bespoke standalone surface. ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, use `HeroSection`. ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` ```tsx ``` Do not use `HeroSection` in standard article content modules. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these imports only when the file uses the component: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Remove unused imports before finalising. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster? ) } ``` If an article uses an internal link, import React Router’s `Link` exactly: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path must follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Use kebab-case filenames and slugs. - The route slug must match the article registry entry. - The SEO config path must match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal links must use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Follow existing registry and SEO structures rather than inventing export names: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` - Use Australian English. - Qualify date-sensitive claims, for example: “as at 2026”. --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI or content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Layout-rendered titles, breadcrumbs, and author sections are not duplicated. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs are used. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text; - purple and orange use white text; - transparent panels use gray or black text. - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Do not imply endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless true. - Explain AI limitations, review needs, privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language where relevant. - Keep startup and funding content educational; do not present financial advice. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add legal, technical, or “Important” disclaimer blocks unless explicitly requested. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” - “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` ### 13.8 Generic CTA section ```tsx ``` ### 13.9 Resource CTA section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` components with `to`. - Use `~/` alias imports, such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes and strong callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for sources. - Use `ArticleResourceList` for resource lists. - Use `ArticleResourceCTA` or `ArticleCompanyCTA` for generic CTAs. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated content wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Do not import or render `ArticleLayout` in article content files. - Do not use Next.js APIs, imports, or directives. - Do not use raw `` for article images. - Do not use `placehold.co`. - Do not hand-code audience card grids. - Do not duplicate headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings rendered by other components. - Do not use unsupported variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Do not use `bg-white` in generated article wrappers. - Do not reference `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Do not add disclaimer blocks or service-chip sections unless explicitly requested. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes The project is Bun-first and uses React Router build/type-generation commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm every import exists and is used. - Confirm every prop matches the component TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports, directives, or APIs appear. - Confirm article route paths, registry slugs, and SEO config paths are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a fragment or valid neutral wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used. - Confirm no raw article image markup or placeholder image URLs are used. - Confirm article content does not import `ArticleLayout`. - If adding `useEffect` cleanup code outside article content, return a void cleanup function body rather than an expression result.
runContext.company_context:
# Company Overview: MLAI

> This document provides context for AI-generated articles. It ensures content aligns with the company's brand, audience, and goals.

---

## 1. Company Identity

**What we are (plain-English):**  
MLAI Aus Inc is a not-for-profit AI and machine learning community based in Australia. It brings together builders, learners, founders, researchers, students, clinicians, data scientists, AI developers, entrepreneurs, and industry supporters through events, hackathons, educational programs, community tools, and startup support initiatives.

**Industry/Sector:** Artificial intelligence, machine learning, startup ecosystem, technology education/community  
**Type:** Not-for-profit community organisation with events, educational programs, hackathons, and software-based founder/community tools  
**Founded:** [To be filled]  
**Location/Market:** Australia; broader relevance to “Australia and beyond” where AI community impact is discussed

---

## 2. Mission & North Star

**Mission Statement:**  
MLAI’s mission is to make AI accessible, fun, and impactful while fostering a vibrant Australian community passionate about AI and machine learning.

**Vision/North Star:**  
To make Australia an amazing home for AI and machine learning entrepreneurs and build a thriving AI ecosystem through meetups, hackathons, educational programs, practical community support, and meaningful professional connections.

**Key Values:**
- **Accessibility:** Make AI approachable for learners, founders, students, clinicians, and practitioners.
- **Practical impact:** Focus on real-world challenges, useful projects, and applied AI rather than abstract hype.
- **Community contribution:** Recognise members who help, volunteer, mentor, support founders, and share updates.
- **Collaboration:** Bring together people from different backgrounds — developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, healthcare professionals, students, sponsors, and mentors.
- **Transparency and momentum:** Encourage founders to build trust through consistent updates, clear asks, and visible progress.

---

## 3. Target Audience

### Primary Audience

Use MLAI as the “home base” in articles for readers such as:

- **AI and machine learning learners:** Students, early-career professionals, and curious beginners who want to understand AI, attend events, meet peers, and build confidence.
- **Builders and practitioners:** Data scientists, AI developers, software engineers, researchers, and technical contributors looking for practical challenges, hackathons, workshops, community support, and collaboration.
- **Startup founders and entrepreneurs:** Founders using AI or building technology companies who need pitch support, investor update habits, community feedback, introductions, and fundraising preparation.
- **Healthcare and medtech innovators:** Clinicians, doctors, nurses, pharmacists, healthcare professionals, students, data scientists, and AI builders interested in solving real healthcare bottlenecks through events such as MedHack.
- **Sponsors and ecosystem partners:** Organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Pain Points to Address

- AI can feel intimidating, abstract, or hard to start with.
- Builders need real-world problems, collaborators, mentors, and structured opportunities to apply their skills.
- Founders often start raising money too late and do not build investor trust early enough.
- Early-stage founders struggle to communicate momentum, metrics, asks, risks, and updates clearly.
- Healthcare systems face bottlenecks around ageing populations, inpatient wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural care, and preventative medicine.
- Community members want simple ways to get involved, contribute meaningfully, and be recognised for helping.
- Sponsors need credible, values-aligned ways to support and reach the Australian AI ecosystem.

### Goals They’re Trying to Achieve

- Learn AI and machine learning in a practical, community-supported environment.
- Meet collaborators, mentors, investors, founders, and technical peers.
- Build prototypes and solve real-world AI challenges through hackathons.
- Improve pitch clarity and communicate ideas effectively.
- Build investor relationships months before a raise.
- Turn community contribution into tangible perks such as coworking, events, mentoring, merch, and office hours.
- Support Australia’s AI and ML ecosystem through sponsorship, partnerships, and community involvement.

---

## 4. Product/Service Offerings

Think of MLAI’s offering as a community ecosystem made up of events, tools, hackathons, and contribution loops.

### Core Offerings

1. **AI and machine learning events:**  
   Meetups, workshops, social events, lightning talks, and educational gatherings across Australia for builders, learners, founders, and practitioners.

2. **Hackathons:**  
   Practical AI challenge events, including upcoming hackathons such as MedHack: Frontiers and the eSafety Hackathon. These events bring participants together to prototype solutions to real-world problems.

3. **MedHack: Frontiers:**  
   A healthcare AI hackathon focused on applying AI to pressing healthcare challenges. It brings clinicians, builders, data scientists, AI developers, mentors, judges, investors, and students together to prototype solutions across streams such as ageing populations, wards, workforce load, emergency departments, rural healthcare, and preventative medicine.

4. **MLAI Vibe Raising:**  
   A founder tool that helps startups draft monthly investor updates from information the founder chooses to provide or optionally connect. It is designed to help founders build investor relationships before they raise by turning wins, asks, challenges, metrics, risks, and follow-ups into visible momentum.

5. **Roo and Roo Points:**  
   Roo is MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks. Roo Points are MLAI’s internal thank-you system for meaningful contributions and can be used for rewards such as coworking, event tickets, office hours, mentoring, merch, and other community perks.

6. **Founder education and resources:**  
   Guides and content such as “How to Pitch Your Big Idea” that help founders communicate complex ideas, use frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution, and close with clear calls to action.

7. **Sponsorship and partnerships:**  
   Sponsorship opportunities for organisations that want to support Australia’s AI community and connect with developers, researchers, entrepreneurs, and builders.

### Key Differentiators

- **Community-first, not just content-first:** MLAI is positioned around active participation, contribution, Slack involvement, volunteering, events, and practical support.
- **Practical AI focus:** Events and hackathons are tied to real-world challenges, not just theory or hype.
- **Cross-disciplinary collaboration:** MLAI brings together technical people, founders, students, researchers, clinicians, mentors, investors, and sponsors.
- **Founder support integrated into an AI community:** MLAI supports not only AI learning and building, but also startup pitching, investor updates, fundraising readiness, and founder visibility.
- **Contribution recognition through Roo Points:** MLAI has an internal system for recognising meaningful community contributions and turning them into practical perks.
- **Australian ecosystem focus:** The brand is explicitly focused on supporting Australia’s AI and ML community and making Australia a stronger home for AI entrepreneurship.

---

## 5. Proof Points & Credibility

**Trust Signals — pick 1–2 per article, max:**
- MLAI is a not-for-profit community based in Australia.
- MLAI has “over 10,000 members” from diverse backgrounds including data scientists, AI developers, healthcare professionals, and students.
- MLAI events are described as “always oversubscribed,” reflecting strong community demand.
- MLAI runs monthly lightning talks, meetups, workshops, hackathons, and educational programs.
- MedHack: Frontiers is run in collaboration with MYMI, a student-led organisation affiliated with Monash University.
- MedHack: Frontiers uses Monash University Clayton Campus as a venue.
- MLAI positions itself as supporting Australia’s AI future and connecting sponsors with developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.
- MLAI Vibe Raising has privacy and terms pages covering Google OAuth, Gmail data use, connected sources, AI-assisted monthly founder updates, retention, deletion, and Limited Use commitments.

**What NOT to claim:**
- Do not claim MLAI is a for-profit SaaS company; it is MLAI Aus Inc and is described as not-for-profit/non-profit.
- Do not claim Roo Points are money, wages, an exchange rate, or a guaranteed pathway to paid work. The site explicitly says they are internal community reward points with no cash value.
- Do not claim Vibe Raising provides financial, legal, tax, investment, medical, or professional advice.
- Do not claim AI-generated outputs from MLAI tools are always accurate; the terms state outputs may be inaccurate, incomplete, outdated, or misleading and must be reviewed.
- Do not claim MLAI sells personal information, Gmail data, Google user data, or connected-source data; the privacy policy says it does not.
- Do not imply Gmail or connected-data access is mandatory for Vibe Raising; connectors are optional.
- Do not invent sponsor names, award wins, funding numbers, partner lists, or member statistics beyond “over 10,000 members.”
- Do not claim participation in events, Roo Points, or volunteering guarantees jobs, team roles, event ownership, investor introductions, funding, or paid work.
- Do not overstate geographic reach beyond the stated Australian focus, though the site does mention “Australia and beyond” in relation to AI impact.

---

## 6. CTA Guidance

### How to Describe the Company in One Line

Choose one per article:

- “MLAI is a not-for-profit Australian community helping people learn, build, and connect around AI and machine learning.”
- “MLAI brings Australia’s AI builders, learners, founders, researchers, and partners together through events, hackathons, tools, and community programs.”
- “MLAI is an Australian AI and machine learning community focused on practical learning, real-world projects, and meaningful professional connections.”
- “MLAI supports Australia’s AI ecosystem with meetups, hackathons, founder tools, educational resources, and community contribution programs.”

### Primary CTAs

Use ONE per article, aligned to topic.

**For beginners/learners:**
> “Join an upcoming MLAI event to meet Australia’s AI and ML community and start learning with other builders.”  
> Target: `/events`

**For builders/practitioners:**
> “Explore MLAI hackathons and build practical AI solutions with other developers, researchers, founders, and domain experts.”  
> Target: `/hackathons` or relevant hackathon page such as `/medhack`

**For healthcare/medtech readers:**
> “Learn more about MedHack: Frontiers and how clinicians, builders, and AI practitioners are tackling real healthcare bottlenecks.”  
> Target: `/medhack`

**For startup founders:**
> “Use MLAI Vibe Raising to draft monthly investor updates and build investor relationships before you raise.”  
> Target: `/vibe-raising`

**For community members:**
> “Meet Roo, MLAI’s Slack helper for getting involved, earning Roo Points, and unlocking community perks.”  
> Target: `/roo`

**For decision-makers/leaders:**
> “Connect with MLAI’s Australian AI community through events, hackathons, and educational programs.”  
> Target: `/events` or `/sponsors`, depending on intent

**For partners/sponsors:**
> “Partner with MLAI to support Australia’s AI community and connect with talented developers, researchers, and entrepreneurs.”  
> Target: `/sponsors`

### URL Patterns or Pages to Link To

- Events: `https://mlai.au/events`
- Hackathons: `https://mlai.au/hackathons`
- MedHack: `https://mlai.au/medhack`
- Vibe Raising: `https://mlai.au/vibe-raising`
- Roo: `https://mlai.au/roo`
- Sponsors: `https://mlai.au/sponsors`
- Privacy policy for Vibe Raising / Google and Gmail data use: `https://mlai.au/privacy`
- Terms of service for Vibe Raising and connected-source use: `https://mlai.au/terms`
- Pitching guide: `https://mlai.au/how-to-pitch-your-idea`

### CTA Rules

- Use ONE CTA per article, either as a natural aside near the relevant section or as a final helpful next step.
- Match the CTA to the article’s topic and reader persona.
- Keep CTAs practical and community-oriented, not sales-heavy.
- For founder/fundraising articles, point to Vibe Raising or the pitching guide.
- For AI learning, community, and industry articles, point to events.
- For healthcare AI articles, point to MedHack.
- For sponsorship, ecosystem, or partnership articles, point to the sponsors page.
- For community participation and contribution articles, point to Roo.
- Mention privacy, optional connectors, and review requirements when discussing Vibe Raising in any data-sensitive context.

### What NOT to do with CTAs

- Do not use hard-sell language like “Buy now,” “Guarantee funding,” “Secure investors instantly,” or “Get paid through Roo Points.”
- Do not imply MLAI provides regulated professional advice.
- Do not push Vibe Raising as an automated replacement for founder judgement, investor relations, accounting, legal review, or financial advice.
- Do not direct every article to Vibe Raising; many MLAI readers are learners, builders, sponsors, or community members rather than founders.
- Do not promise event availability, acceptance, perks, mentoring, coworking, or rewards if capacity is limited.
- Do not imply joining Slack, attending an event, or earning Roo Points guarantees employment, sponsorship, investment, or leadership roles.

---

## 7. Tone & Voice Guidelines

**Brand Voice:**  
Practical, community-first, builder-friendly, accessible, optimistic, and slightly informal. MLAI’s tone is energetic and helpful without being overly corporate. It should feel like a capable community organiser speaking to founders, builders, learners, and partners.

### Tone Attributes

- **Practical over theoretical:** Focus on what people can build, learn, attend, draft, improve, or contribute.
- **Community-first:** Use language that invites participation and recognises contribution.
- **Accessible and encouraging:** Make AI feel approachable, especially for beginners and cross-disciplinary audiences.
- **Optimistic but grounded:** Celebrate AI’s potential while staying focused on real-world problems and responsible use.
- **Founder-aware:** For startup content, emphasise momentum, transparency, clear asks, investor trust, and starting early.
- **Technically accurate but not exclusionary:** Explain concepts clearly without dumbing them down or turning articles into jargon walls.

### Do’s

- Use plain-English explanations before technical depth.
- Use Australian context where relevant, especially for AI careers, events, community, startups, healthcare, and policy.
- Use community language such as “builders,” “learners,” “founders,” “members,” “mentors,” “contributors,” and “AI community.”
- Highlight practical formats: meetups, workshops, lightning talks, hackathons, Slack, founder updates, pitch nights, coworking, mentoring.
- When discussing pitching, use direct, useful frameworks such as Problem-Agitate-Solution and clear calls to action.
- When discussing Vibe Raising, mention that founders can optionally connect Gmail or other sources and must review AI-generated outputs.
- When discussing Roo Points, describe them as internal community recognition or thank-you points, not money.
- Use clear CTAs that invite readers to attend, join, build, contribute, draft, learn, or partner.

### Don’ts

- Don’t sound like a generic enterprise AI vendor.
- Don’t overhype AI as a magic solution.
- Don’t present MLAI as a university, accelerator, recruitment agency, investment fund, or consulting firm unless future materials explicitly say so.
- Don’t imply MLAI owns or operates Monash University, MYMI, Google, Slack, Xero, Linear, Notion, or other third-party services.
- Don’t write in a patronising tone toward beginners.
- Don’t frame community contribution as unpaid labour-for-wages; Roo Points are recognition and perks, not compensation.
- Don’t make guarantees around fundraising, jobs, investment, AI accuracy, hackathon outcomes, or event acceptance.
- Don’t use excessive corporate buzzwords such as “synergistic transformation,” “AI revolution platform,” or “end-to-end disruption engine.”

---

## 8. Writing Guidance Summary

### Language Conventions

- **Locale:** Australian English preferred for general articles, given the Australian focus. Preserve official names and quoted website wording where needed.
- **Preferred terms:**  
  AI, machine learning, AI community, Australian AI community, builders, learners, founders, contributors, mentors, hackathons, meetups, workshops, lightning talks, Roo, Roo Points, Vibe Raising, monthly investor updates, founder updates, meaningful contribution, community perks.
- **Avoided terms:**  
  Guaranteed funding, guaranteed job, cash value, wage, exchange rate, investment advice, legal advice, medical advice, professional advice, fully automated investor relations, instant investor access, replacement for review, AI certainty.

### Content Rules

- Position MLAI as a not-for-profit Australian AI and machine learning community, not as a generic SaaS vendor.
- Keep the core message practical: learn, build, connect, contribute, and solve real problems.
- Use the “over 10,000 members” proof point sparingly and accurately.
- For founder articles, emphasise building investor relationships before raising, monthly updates, transparency, metrics, asks, risks, and momentum.
- For healthcare AI articles, connect content to real healthcare bottlenecks and cross-disciplinary collaboration between clinicians and builders.
- For Roo-related articles, explain that Roo lives in Slack and helps manage contribution recognition, rewards, coworking, requests, and admin flows.
- For Vibe Raising articles, state that connected data sources such as Gmail are optional and that users choose what to provide or connect.
- Always include the review caveat for AI-assisted outputs when relevant.
- Avoid implying that MLAI’s tools, events, or community programs guarantee outcomes.
- Do not invent details about sponsorship tiers, sponsors, awards, leadership, founding year, funding, or event attendance numbers.
- Keep CTAs light, useful, and aligned to the reader’s likely next step.

org_config_snapshot.design_guide:
# MLAI — Brand & Codebase Design Guide — React Router Article Surfaces Version: 2.0 — Scope: branding, Tailwind tokens, article component usage, import paths, routing, accessibility, and one-shot article generation rules. Audience: Engineers, designers, AI agents. Goal: Build UI and long-form article content that compiles first try in the MLAI codebase. Applies to: article content under `app/articles/content/**`, article support components under `app/components/articles/**`, standalone page headers, and reusable resource/CTA blocks. --- ## 1) Current framework and architecture ### 1.1 Framework This repository is a **React Router v7 + React 19 + Vite + TypeScript** application. Observed versions: ```txt react: 19.2.1 react-router: 7.10.0 vite: 7.3.1 typescript: 5.9.3 tailwindcss: 4.1.17 ``` Use React Router links: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles ``` Do not use Next.js APIs or imports: ```tsx import Link from 'next/link' import Image from 'next/image' ``` Do not use: - `use client` - `use server` - `export const metadata` - `generateStaticParams` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - Next.js App Router conventions - Vue or Svelte syntax ### 1.2 Article architecture Article content files live under: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` Article content modules export a default React component: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* Article body only. */} ) } ``` Critical article rules: - **Do not import or wrap content in `ArticleLayout`.** The route handler/layout already renders article framing. - Do not add duplicate article titles, hero sections, breadcrumbs, dates, or author sections when the layout renders them. - Content should return a fragment (`<>...`) or a neutral wrapper. - If a wrapper is necessary, use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`. - Use existing article components rather than ad-hoc card, callout, or image markup. - Do not use placeholder services such as `placehold.co`. - Use real image URLs, supported component fallback behaviour, or `` only in templates. - Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` unless explicitly requested. - Registry route slugs and SEO-config paths must match exactly. --- ## 2) Brand identity ### 2.1 Palette and observed codebase colours The article system combines MLAI CSS variables with bold article surfaces. | Token / colour | Usage | Role | |---|---|---| | `--brand` | `ArticleStepList` bullets and borders | MLAI teal accent | | `--brand-ink` | Link hover states | Teal link colour | | `--accent` | Step-list bullet text | Dark text on brand surfaces | | `--soft` | Existing legacy `ArticleCallout` styling | Soft teal panel | | `--brutalist-orange` | Article search filters | Orange token | | `--brutalist-purple` | Article search filters | Purple token | | `--brutalist-black` | Article search filters | Black token | | `--brutalist-yellow` | Article search filters | Yellow token | | `--brutalist-beige` | `HeroSection` beige variant | Beige page surface | | `#ff3d00` | Audience cards, quotes, CTAs | Orange | | `#4b1bd1` | Audience cards and quotes | Purple | | `#ffe900` | Audience cards | Yellow | | `#2b0f70` | Resource CTA | Deep purple | | `#0d9488` | Resource CTA | Teal/cyan | ### 2.2 Colour guardrails Use existing patterns: - Purple surfaces: `bg-[#4b1bd1] text-white` - Orange surfaces: `bg-[#ff3d00] text-white` - Yellow surfaces: `bg-[#ffe900] text-black` - Transparent article panels: `bg-transparent border border-gray-300` or `border-gray-400` - Links on light backgrounds: `text-[--brand-ink] underline underline-offset-4` - Neutral article text: `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700`, `text-gray-600` Do not: - Put `text-white` on yellow. - Use `bg-white` for generated article content wrappers. - Invent new colours or variants for article design-system components. - Use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` in generated article content. - Overuse bright surfaces in long reading sections. --- ## 3) Typography, spacing, and shape The codebase uses Tailwind utilities and `@tailwindcss/typography`. Common article surface shapes: ```tsx rounded-[22px] rounded-[28px] rounded-[32px] rounded-2xl rounded-3xl ``` Common spacing: ```tsx my-8 my-12 mt-8 mt-10 mt-12 p-6 p-7 p-8 sm:p-7 md:p-9 ``` Common text classes: ```tsx text-gray-900 text-gray-800 text-gray-700 text-gray-600 text-sm text-base text-lg text-xl text-2xl text-3xl sm:text-4xl font-bold ``` --- ## 4) Styling system ### 4.1 Tailwind setup Tailwind scans application code under `app/` and uses the typography plugin: ```ts import typography from '@tailwindcss/typography' import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss' export default { content: ['./app/**/{**,.client,.server}/**/*.{js,jsx,ts,tsx}'], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [typography], } satisfies Config ``` Use Tailwind classes directly in TSX. Do not introduce CSS modules or styled-components for generated article work. ### 4.2 Import convention Use the configured `~/` alias for application imports: ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Use relative imports only when modifying an existing nearby module that already uses them. --- ## 5) Routing and links Internal links use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' Articles Read more ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx External source ``` Do not use `href` on React Router `Link`; use `to`. --- ## 6) Article component rules Use existing article design-system components. Do not recreate their functionality with raw bordered cards, quote boxes, or image blocks. ### 6.1 Persona and audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` Allowed card variants: ```tsx 'orange' | 'purple' | 'yellow' ``` ```tsx ``` Do not hand-code audience cards with raw `
` border or shadow markup. --- ### 6.2 Quotes and callouts Use `QuoteBlock` for strong callouts and quotations. ```tsx import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'purple' | 'orange' ``` ```tsx AI adoption works best when the team starts with a clear workflow, a measurable outcome, and a review loop for quality and risk. ``` Do not use unsupported generated-content variants: ```tsx ``` Do not use `ArticleCallout` for newly generated article callouts. Use `QuoteBlock`. --- ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock`, not raw `` markup. ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleImageBlockProps = { src: string alt: string width?: number height?: number containerClassName?: string imageClassName?: string caption?: string } ``` `ArticleImageBlock` uses `ImageWithFallback` and `ARTICLE_FALLBACK_IMAGE` internally. Do not use: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.4 FAQ Use `ArticleFAQ`. ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'How should a small team start with AI?', answer: 'Start with one workflow, define the outcome, and review outputs before scaling.', }, { question: 'Should every AI task be automated?', answer: 'Not necessarily. High-impact or high-risk decisions should retain human review, especially where accuracy, privacy, or customer trust matters.', }, ] ``` `ArticleFAQ` renders its own heading. Do not add an identical heading directly before it. Supported item keys: ```ts export type ArticleFAQItem = { q?: string question?: string a?: ReactNode answer?: ReactNode } ``` Prefer `question` and `answer`. Keep answers as simple inline content because the component renders answers inside a paragraph. --- ### 6.5 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList`. ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` For generated content, use `accent="teal"`: ```tsx ``` Steps may be strings or `{ label: string }` objects. Prefer strings for standard generated articles. --- ### 6.6 Table-of-contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` only where the article enhancer/layout is expected to populate it. ```tsx import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` ```tsx ``` For no external margin: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.7 References Use `ArticleReferences`. ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' ``` Supported categories: ```tsx 'government' | 'guide' | 'analysis' | 'industry' | 'watchlist' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleReferencesProps = { references: ReferenceItem[] heading?: string description?: string headingId?: string previewCount?: number } ``` --- ### 6.8 Resource lists Use `ArticleResourceList`. ```tsx import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' ``` Supported badge tones: ```tsx 'red' | 'purple' | 'black' | 'yellow' ``` ```tsx const resources: ArticleResource[] = [ { label: 'AI adoption checklist', description: 'A practical checklist for scoping, testing, and reviewing AI use cases.', href: '/articles', ctaLabel: 'Open', type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Free', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Do not reference unavailable components: ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.9 Article CTA blocks #### Simple company CTA ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts export interface ArticleCompanyCTAProps { title: string body: string buttonText: string buttonHref: string note?: string id?: string className?: string } ``` Keep CTA copy generic. Do not hard-code product names unless explicitly requested. #### Resource CTA ```tsx import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' ``` Allowed accents: ```tsx 'purple' | 'cyan' | 'orange' ``` ```tsx ``` --- ### 6.10 Related article cards Use `ArticleCardBySlug` when linking to a registered article. ```tsx import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' ``` ```tsx ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'default' | 'compact' ``` Supported props: ```ts type Props = { slug: string ctaText?: string className?: string descriptionOverride?: string variant?: 'default' | 'compact' authorOverride?: ArticleAuthor } ``` The component resolves article metadata and route slugs internally. --- ### 6.11 Footer article navigation Use `ArticleFooterNav` only where the article pattern needs in-body footer navigation and the layout does not already provide it. ```tsx import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' ``` ```tsx ``` Supported props: ```ts type ArticleFooterNavProps = { backHref?: string backLabel?: string topHref?: string className?: string } ``` --- ### 6.12 Author bio Use `AuthorBio` only when an in-body author section is required and the layout does not already render author information. ```tsx import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' ``` ```tsx ``` Avoid duplicate author blocks. --- ## 7) Page header components ### 7.1 Article body files Article content files should not manually render an article hero/header because `ArticleLayout` handles article framing. Do not import: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' ``` Do not wrap content in: ```tsx ... ``` Do not render `ArticleHeroHeader` in generated article body files unless explicitly requested for a bespoke standalone surface. ### 7.2 Standalone page hero For non-article standalone pages, use `HeroSection`. ```tsx import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Allowed variants: ```tsx 'yellow' | 'beige' | 'white' ``` ```tsx ``` Do not use `HeroSection` in standard article content modules. --- ## 8) Component imports — approved quick reference Use these imports only when the file uses the component: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleTocPlaceholder } from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleResourceList, type ArticleResource, } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import { ArticleResourceCTA } from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceCTA' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleCardBySlug from '~/components/articles/ArticleCardBySlug' import { ArticleFooterNav } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFooterNav' import AuthorBio from '~/components/AuthorBio' import HeroSection from '~/components/ui/HeroSection' ``` Remove unused imports before finalising. --- ## 9) Article file one-shot skeleton Use this structure for a new article content module: ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { QuoteBlock } from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: 'What is the best first step?', answer: 'Start with a narrow workflow, a clear success measure, and a human review process.', }, ] const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI information', publisher: 'Department of Industry, Science and Resources', category: 'government', }, ] export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

Start with a concise introduction that explains the practical problem, who the guide is for, and what the reader will be able to do next.

Start with the workflow

AI decisions are easier when the team starts from the workflow instead of the tool. Map the current process, identify the bottleneck, and decide what a useful improvement would look like.

Treat AI as a workflow capability, not a magic feature. The useful question is: what decision, draft, search, summary, or review step can become clearer or faster? ) } ``` If an article uses an internal link, import React Router’s `Link` exactly: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' ``` --- ## 10) Metadata, registry, and SEO consistency When adding or updating an article: - Content file path must follow: ```txt app/articles/content/{category}/{slug}.tsx ``` - Use kebab-case filenames and slugs. - The route slug must match the article registry entry. - The SEO config path must match the registry slug path exactly. - Internal links must use the final public route: ```tsx ... ``` - Follow existing registry and SEO structures rather than inventing export names: ```txt app/articles/registry app/articles/seo-config ``` - Use Australian English. - Qualify date-sensitive claims, for example: “as at 2026”. --- ## 11) Accessibility checklist Apply to every generated UI or content change: - [ ] Headings follow semantic order. - [ ] Layout-rendered titles, breadcrumbs, and author sections are not duplicated. - [ ] Link text is descriptive. - [ ] External links use `target="_blank"` and `rel="noopener noreferrer"`. - [ ] Images have meaningful `alt` text unless decorative. - [ ] No placeholder image URLs are used. - [ ] Colour contrast is appropriate: - yellow uses black text; - purple and orange use white text; - transparent panels use gray or black text. - [ ] Interactive elements have visible focus styles. - [ ] Icon-only controls include `aria-label`. - [ ] Lists use semantic `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` markup. --- ## 12) Copy and content rules Use the MLAI tone: - Australian English. - Practical, clear, and educational. - Define acronyms once. - Avoid hype, guarantees, and exaggerated claims. - Do not imply endorsement by government, universities, vendors, accelerators, or investors unless true. - Explain AI limitations, review needs, privacy concerns, and verification steps in plain language where relevant. - Keep startup and funding content educational; do not present financial advice. - Do not add medical disclaimers. - Do not add legal, technical, or “Important” disclaimer blocks unless explicitly requested. - Do not add domain-specific service chips or “browse services by need” tag groups. - Keep CTA text generic, for example: - “Get recommendations” - “Explore resources” - “Read the guide” - “Plan your next step” - “Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}” --- ## 13) Patterns and recipes ### 13.1 Internal article cross-link ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' how to get started with AI ``` ### 13.2 External source link ```tsx Australian Government AI resources ``` ### 13.3 Persona section ```tsx ``` ### 13.4 Strong callout ```tsx If the workflow requires judgement, accountability, or customer trust, keep a human review step. ``` ### 13.5 Image block ```tsx ``` ### 13.6 Step-by-step section ```tsx ``` ### 13.7 References section ```tsx ``` ### 13.8 Generic CTA section ```tsx ``` ### 13.9 Resource CTA section ```tsx ``` --- ## 14) Do / Don’t quick list Do: - Use `react-router` `Link` components with `to`. - Use `~/` alias imports, such as `~/components/articles/QuoteBlock`. - Use `AudienceGrid` for persona cards. - Use `QuoteBlock` for quotes and strong callouts. - Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article images. - Use `ArticleFAQ` for FAQs. - Use `ArticleReferences` for sources. - Use `ArticleResourceList` for resource lists. - Use `ArticleResourceCTA` or `ArticleCompanyCTA` for generic CTAs. - Use `bg-transparent` for generated content wrappers. - Keep article files as body content only. - Remove unused imports. Don’t: - Do not import or render `ArticleLayout` in article content files. - Do not use Next.js APIs, imports, or directives. - Do not use raw `` for article images. - Do not use `placehold.co`. - Do not hand-code audience card grids. - Do not duplicate headers, breadcrumbs, author bios, or FAQ headings rendered by other components. - Do not use unsupported variants such as `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"`. - Do not use `bg-white` in generated article wrappers. - Do not reference `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. - Do not add disclaimer blocks or service-chip sections unless explicitly requested. --- ## 15) Build and validation notes The project is Bun-first and uses React Router build/type-generation commands. Relevant scripts: ```txt bun run build bun run typecheck bun run check:article-internal-links ``` Before finalising generated code: - Confirm every import exists and is used. - Confirm every prop matches the component TypeScript type. - Confirm no Next.js imports, directives, or APIs appear. - Confirm article route paths, registry slugs, and SEO config paths are consistent. - Confirm JSX returns a fragment or valid neutral wrapper. - Confirm no unsupported variants are used. - Confirm no raw article image markup or placeholder image URLs are used. - Confirm article content does not import `ArticleLayout`. - If adding `useEffect` cleanup code outside article content, return a void cleanup function body rather than an expression result.

# EXISTING ARTIFACT ```markdown MLAI — One-Shot React Router Article/Resource Generation Spec (TSX) — v2026-03-08 Purpose. Hand this document directly to an AI agent as a system/developer prompt so the agent can generate MLAI article/resource content for the current `mlai-au` codebase in one shot. The output must compile in the React Router v7 + React 19.2.1 + TypeScript + Tailwind project without manual corrections. MLAI context. MLAI is a not-for-profit community organisation helping Australians learn about startups and artificial intelligence. Resources should be practical, inclusive, factual, Australian-context aware, and date-qualified where helpful. Avoid hype, guarantees, exaggerated claims, and unsupported statistics. Output format: `.tsx` article content module(s) for the existing React Router codebase. Framework: React Router v7.10.0, React 19.2.1, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS. Package/runtime: Bun-first project. Build/typecheck commands use `bun run typecheck` and `bun run build`. Do not generate a PDF. Do not use ReportLab. Do not use Next.js patterns. ## 0) Quick-start contract for the agent Task: Given a Title, Slug, Category, Description, Date, optional Hero Image, article body outline/sections, FAQs, references, and optional CTA requirements, generate a TypeScript React article content file that adheres 100% to the rules below. Primary file path: ```txt app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx ``` The file must: - Use `.tsx` - Default export a function component named `ArticlePage` - Return article body content only - NOT import or wrap in `ArticleLayout` - NOT render a duplicate article hero, title, breadcrumb, author, date, or header because `ArticleLayout` is applied by the route layer - Use React Router links from `react-router` - Use actual codebase components from `~/components/articles/...` - Use Tailwind utility classes and existing project CSS variables - Compile with TypeScript - Include only imports that are actually used - Use `bg-transparent`, never `bg-white`, for any generated wrapper Article QA: the article must render in local development, production build, and the deployed React Router route without hydration errors, broken imports, invalid props, duplicated headers, or registry/SEO path inconsistencies. Deliverables: - `app/articles/content/{category-slug}/{article-slug}.tsx` — primary article content module - Registry/SEO updates only if the existing repository pattern requires them: - `app/articles/registry.ts` - `app/articles/seo-config.ts` - Do not invent new shared components or unsupported props. ## 1) Inputs you will receive Supply at minimum: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: string, slug: string, categorySlug: string, description: string, datePublished: string, // ISO date, e.g. "2026-01-15" heroImage?: string, // use a real URL or "", never placehold.co heroImageAlt?: string, sections: Array<{ heading: string, body: string, subsections?: Array<{ heading: string, body: string }> }>, audienceCards?: Array<{ title: string, description: string, variant: "orange" | "purple" | "yellow" }>, quoteBlocks?: Array<{ title?: string, variant: "purple" | "orange", body: string }>, images?: Array<{ src: string, alt: string, caption?: string }>, faqs?: Array<{ question: string, answer: string }>, references?: Array<{ href: string, title: string, publisher: string, description?: string, category?: "government" | "guide" | "analysis" | "industry" | "watchlist" }>, cta?: { title: string, body: string, buttonText: string, buttonHref: string, note?: string } } ``` Example input schema for planning only: ```ts { topic: "{{TOPIC}}", title: "How to learn AI in Australia", slug: "how-to-learn-ai-in-australia", categorySlug: "ai-learning-resources", description: "A practical guide to learning AI in Australia, including community, projects, and credible resources.", datePublished: "2026-01-15", heroImage: "", heroImageAlt: "People learning artificial intelligence together", audienceCards: [ { title: "Students", description: "People exploring AI, machine learning, and data skills for study or early career decisions.", variant: "orange" }, { title: "Career switchers", description: "Professionals who want a practical path into AI without starting from scratch.", variant: "purple" }, { title: "Founders and builders", description: "Startup-minded learners who want to apply AI to real products and workflows.", variant: "yellow" } ], cta: { title: "Get recommendations for {{TOPIC}}", body: "Explore practical MLAI resources and community pathways related to this topic.", buttonText: "Browse related resources", buttonHref: "/articles" } } ``` ## 2) Framework, routing, and project configuration Framework detection is authoritative: - Framework: React Router v7.10.0 - Rendering library: React 19.2.1 - Language: TypeScript - Component extension: `.tsx` - Styling: Tailwind CSS with `@tailwindcss/typography` and existing CSS variables - Build tool: Vite - Router imports: `react-router` - Link component: `...` - Import alias: `~/` maps to `app/` - Package manager/runtime: Bun Never use: - `next/link` - `next/image` - `next/dynamic` - `next/font` - `'use client'` - `'use server'` - `getStaticProps` - `getServerSideProps` - `generateStaticParams` - `export const metadata` - Vue or Svelte syntax - CSS modules or styled-components Use these actual project conventions: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' import { ArticleCallout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleCallout' import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' import ArticleResourceList from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock' ``` Import ordering: 1. React/type imports, only if actually used 2. React Router imports, only if internal links are used 3. Icon imports, only if actually used 4. Internal component imports 5. Article utilities/authors, only if used Generated article content files use no semicolons. ## 3) File path and export rules Primary article content file: ```txt app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx ``` Example: ```txt app/articles/content/ai-learning-resources/how-to-learn-ai-in-australia.tsx ``` Required component export: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <> {/* article body only */} ) } ``` Content files must NOT do this: ```tsx import { ArticleLayout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleLayout' export default function ArticlePage() { return ( ... ) } ``` `ArticleLayout` is handled by the route/registry layer. Importing or wrapping with `ArticleLayout` in a content file is a build and design failure. Do not invent a metadata API. Only add local metadata constants when nearby content files use them or when explicitly required. If updating `app/articles/registry.ts` or `app/articles/seo-config.ts`, follow the exact existing object shape in those files. Path consistency is mandatory: - Registry slug/route must match the content file slug. - SEO config path must match the registry route path exactly. - Article routes use: ```txt /articles/{slug} ``` - Article content uses: ```txt app/articles/content/{categorySlug}/{slug}.tsx ``` ## 4) Header, title, hero, and layout rules No duplicate headers. `ArticleLayout` already handles the surrounding article presentation. The content module must not duplicate title, breadcrumbs, author, date, hero, SEO header, disclaimer, or layout chrome. Content files should begin with article body content: ```tsx export default function ArticlePage() { return ( <>

...

...

... ) } ``` Do not use `ArticleHeroHeader` in standard article content modules. It renders its own breadcrumbs, title, hero image, and header presentation, which duplicates layout-owned content. Only use `ArticleHeroHeader` when an existing custom-header article pattern explicitly requires it. If used, follow the established custom-header convention exactly, including any required flag such as: ```tsx export const useCustomHeader = true ``` Its actual import and valid props are: ```tsx import { ArticleHeroHeader } from '~/components/articles/ArticleHeroHeader' ``` Valid `headerBgColor` values only: - `cyan` - `purple` - `blue` - `orange` ## 5) Styling and brand rules Styling approach: Tailwind utilities and existing project CSS variables. Use actual classes and tokens found in the project, including: - `bg-transparent` - `border-gray-300` - `border-gray-400` - `text-gray-900` - `text-gray-800` - `text-gray-700` - `text-gray-600` - `text-gray-500` - `text-[--brand-ink]` - `bg-[--brand]` - `bg-[--soft]` - `bg-[#ff3d00]` - `bg-[#4b1bd1]` - `bg-[#ffe900]` - `bg-[#2b0f70]` - `bg-[#0d9488]` - `rounded-[22px]` - `rounded-[28px]` - `rounded-[32px]` - `rounded-2xl` - `rounded-3xl` - `my-8` - `my-12` - `mt-8` - `mt-10` - `mb-6` - `space-y-3` - `space-y-6` - `grid gap-4 sm:gap-6 md:grid-cols-3` Generated wrappers must use: ```tsx
...
``` Never generate a wrapper with `bg-white`. Existing shared components may manage their own internal backgrounds; do not rewrite their internals. ## 6) Design system component rules Use existing article components instead of raw HTML UI blocks. Do not invent components or props. These components are not available: - `ArticleResourceDownload` - `ArticleSummaryCard` ### 6.1 Persona / audience cards Use `AudienceGrid`. ```tsx import { AudienceGrid } from '~/components/articles/AudienceGrid' ``` ```tsx ``` Valid `AudienceGrid` variants only: - `orange` - `purple` - `yellow` Never use `blue`, `brand`, or `teal` as an `AudienceGrid` variant. ### 6.2 Callouts / quotes / highlighted notes Use `QuoteBlock` for narrative callouts, highlighted takeaways, and quote-style blocks. ```tsx import QuoteBlock from '~/components/articles/QuoteBlock'

...

 ``` Valid `QuoteBlock` variants only: - `purple` - `orange` Never use `variant="blue"` or `variant="brand"` on `QuoteBlock`. Use `ArticleCallout` only where an informational, success, warning, or brand callout is specifically appropriate. ```tsx import { ArticleCallout } from '~/components/articles/ArticleCallout'

...

 ``` Valid `ArticleCallout` variants: - `info` - `success` - `warning` - `brand` Do not import `ArticleDisclaimer` unless explicitly requested. Do not add medical, legal, technical, or other disclaimers by default. ### 6.3 Images Use `ArticleImageBlock` for article body images. ```tsx import { ArticleImageBlock } from '~/components/articles/ArticleImageBlock' ``` Optional props: - `width?: number` - `height?: number` - `containerClassName?: string` - `imageClassName?: string` - `caption?: string` Never use raw `` for article body images when `ArticleImageBlock` fits. Never use `placehold.co`. Use a real production image URL, `''`, or an empty string only where the existing component/registry fallback handles it. ### 6.4 FAQs Use `ArticleFAQ`. ```tsx import { ArticleFAQ, type ArticleFAQItem } from '~/components/articles/ArticleFAQ' ``` Valid item shape: ```tsx const faqItems: ArticleFAQItem[] = [ { question: '...', answer: '...', }, { q: '...', a: '...', }, ] ``` Render: ```tsx ``` Valid props: - `items` - `anchorId` - `heading` `ArticleFAQ` renders answers inside a `

`. Use plain strings or inline React nodes only. Do not pass fragments containing `

`, `

`, `
    `, or other block elements as FAQ answers. ### 6.5 CTA blocks For a generic CTA, use `ArticleCompanyCTA`. ```tsx import ArticleCompanyCTA from '~/components/articles/ArticleCompanyCTA' ``` Optional props: - `note` - `id` - `className` Keep CTA copy generic. Do not use product-specific names unless explicitly requested. Do not add service chips, browse-by-need chips, or unrelated product cards. ### 6.6 Table of contents placeholder Use `ArticleTocPlaceholder` when the article enhancer/layout should inject a table of contents. ```tsx import ArticleTocPlaceholder from '~/components/articles/ArticleTocPlaceholder' ``` Valid props: - `className` - `noMargin` Do not manually create a static table of contents unless the route specifically requires one. ### 6.7 Step lists Use `ArticleStepList` for numbered process lists. ```tsx import { ArticleStepList } from '~/components/articles/ArticleStepList' ``` `steps` can be strings or `{ label: string }` objects. Use only these generated-content `accent` values: - `teal` - `indigo` Do not use `accent="brand"` in generated content. ### 6.8 References Use `ArticleReferences` for references rendered within article content. ```tsx import { ArticleReferences } from '~/components/articles/ArticleReferences' const references = [ { id: 1, href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', title: 'Australian Government AI resources', publisher: 'Australian Government', description: 'Government information relevant to AI adoption and policy.', category: 'government', }, ] ``` ```tsx ``` Optional props: - `headingId` - `previewCount` Preferred categories: - `government` - `guide` - `analysis` - `industry` - `watchlist` ### 6.9 Resource lists For external or downloadable resource lists, use `ArticleResourceList`. ```tsx import ArticleResourceList from '~/components/articles/ArticleResourceList' const resources = [ { label: 'Australian Government AI guidance', description: 'Official guidance relevant to AI adoption and responsible use.', href: 'https://www.industry.gov.au/', ctaLabel: 'Open resource', openInNewTab: true, type: { label: 'Guide', tone: 'yellow' }, status: { label: 'Official', tone: 'purple' }, }, ] ``` Valid props: - `items` - `className` - `itemClassName` - `defaultCtaLabel` Valid explicit badge tones: - `red` - `purple` - `black` - `yellow` Do not invent `ArticleResourceDownload` or `ArticleSummaryCard`. ## 7) Links and navigation Internal links must use React Router: ```tsx import { Link } from 'react-router' machine learning basics ``` External links use plain anchors: ```tsx CSIRO ``` Do not use `href` on React Router ``. Do not use `next/link`. ## 8) Content structure and accessibility Use semantic article body structure: ```tsx <>

    Intro paragraph...

    Why it matters

    ...

    Practical example

    ...

    • ...
    ``` Heading rules: - Use one logical `h2` per major section. - Use `h3` for subsections. - Do not render an `h1` inside standard article content. - Use stable kebab-case `id` values on major `h2` headings for TOC/deep links. - Do not duplicate heading IDs. Accessibility rules: - Link text must identify the destination. - Image alt text must be descriptive. - Do not use emoji as the only label. - Avoid long unbroken URLs in visible text. - External references should identify the publisher/source. ## 9) Tables, grids, and comparison content Prefer prose, lists, and existing components over complex raw tables. If a table is necessary: ```tsx

    Start with a practical question
